description: Wednesday 12 November 2025 | Standing Orders Committee Hearing of evidence | Review of Standing Orders 2026 New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties Andrew Ecclestone, Deputy Chair Franks Ogilvie Stephen Franks, Director Marcus Ganley, Special Counsel Graeme Edgeler Trust Democracy Simon Wright, Committee member Andrew Ecclestone, Committee member Louis Donovan Peter Hoare Jonathan Godfrey Ethan McCormick Craig Atkinson

chunks.text: Right. Uh, Welcome to Table. Uh, we're in your heads. Sure, Koto. My name's Andrew Eton. I'm the Deputy Chair of the New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties and an adjunct senior research fellow at Victoria, university of Wellington and School of Government. Uh, N-Z-T-C-L is a non-partisan voluntary organization established in 1952, which advocates for human rights and the maintenance and civil liberties.

Um, this is our second time we've submitted standing Orders review. The first time was three years ago, and, uh, unfortunately, many of the issues we raised three years ago is still relevant now in our consideration. Um, but we sent a fairly substantial written submission about the issues that concern us, uh, to the committee, and we assume that it's been read.

Um, we, uh, are particularly concerned about the independence of select committees from the executive and for the legislature command public trust and confidence, which is an issue for democracies around the world. It has to be seen to be acting independently. Uh, members of this committee will be aware of the exchanges over the last two days regarding the Chair of the Environment Committee curtailing the time for submissions on the fast track approval of Amendment Bill.

And indeed, I watched the, uh, the exchange at question time today. Um, the, the council's concerned that the chair of the committee allowed the desire of the government to get a bill on the statute book by the end of the year to override normal timeframes for public input on the bill. Uh, it looked to us like it was a, a lighting, if I can put it politely, of the requirement in standing orders for there to be a debate at first reading on report back shorter than four months.

And, uh, in turn, that makes it look like the independence of the select committee processes being compromised, or that there was a desire by the government to avoid debate on a shorter time report back. This suggests to us that standing orders need to be amended to explicitly protect against the chair or committee significantly curtailing IE not reporting back by a day or two early the time for evidence to be submitted and considered by the select committee.

For example, standing order 3 0 3 states that committees must report after six months, unless a shorter period is instructed by the house, and there's no explicit power for a committee to shorten a report back. We think this is a good place for a clarifying amendment to make it clear that shortening consideration below the level agreed by the house requires a motion in the house.

Besides this recent incident, our submission sets out our longstanding concerns regarding analysis of submissions to select committees. Um, in particular, uh, and we state point this out in paragraph 52 of our written submission, David McGee, in the fourth edition of parliamentary practice in New Zealand said that although advisors, although government officials are advisors to the committee, their primary duty of public servants is to their respective ministers.

And it is always for ministers to decide whether to make available officials available as advisors and which officials. So, although they're there to support the committee, their primary duty is to support the government and the government's mission is to get the bill through. Um, we raised this concern in our, uh, submission to the 2023 standing oil was reviewed based on our experience since then, however, at least two select committees have public expressed their unhappiness at government officials acting contrary to committee's wishes, IE the

concerns that we raised. Um, for example, in June, 2023, newsroom reported that the Department of Internal Affairs had acted contrary to the wishes of the Finance and Expenditure Committee. When considering the legislation on three Waters, and the committee's report stated, we wish to put on record that we did experience some problems during our gruesome of the bill and the preparation of this report to the House.

We are concerned that there were instances where it appeared that departmental officials perhaps inadvertently directed Parliamentary council to make changes to the revision track version of the bill that were either not clearly authorized by us or ran contrary to recommendations. And two months later, the Herald reported that MBI had to apologize to the Foreign Affairs Defense and Trade Select Committee after what our chair here described as devious behavior.

The select committee was split, and so did not call for a departmental report in its consideration of the bill, but MB officials nevertheless prepared a report which the immigration minister said he would use to inform any changes. So a Green Party member of that select committee commented on the MB Chief Executive's explanation saying this was not a process error. I think that with respect, you need to stop characterizing in that way.

They knew that the committee did not want to report. This is a real problem in terms of how the public perceive and can have confidence in the legislature acting independently of the executive and standing up for New Zealanders, having their views properly scrutinized and reported when making submissions to a select committee.

Now previously, resourcing for Parliament to employ its own select committee advisors has been hampered by government control of Parliament's budget. But passage of the Parliament Bill changes this so that we think the Standing Orders Review Committee should make recommendations to that the committee that will be considering Parliament budget setting, uh, provide the clerk with funding to employ significantly more officials to guarantee Parliamentary officials provide this analysis to MPS and not government officials.

And we say in our written submission that if the, if this committee doesn't want to go that far, then it should be commissioning the parliamentary library to do comparative research on legislatures around the world, particularly Westminster, but I think also Ireland, the United States to say what resourcing is given to the, the parliamentary officials in those literature so that the independence of select committee scrutiny of bills is protected.

We also talk about the issues in our submission with, uh, the rise of, uh, the use of technology to, to both make submissions on bills and analyze submissions on bills. Um, I won't go into this in great detail because we've, we've set it out on page 17 and following pages of our submission. But we, uh, recommend that, uh, parliament should be exploring alternative uses of technology to build public trust in democratic processes and to facilitate public input in ways that don't lead to the overwhelming committee with tens

of thousands of submissions. Uh, we say you should commission the parliamentary library to provide it with a report on processes and technologies used in other countries to facilitate meaningful public participation in development and scrutiny of legislation. Um, in terms of urgency, you will be unsurprised to hear that we're, uh, quite concerned about the use of it, uh, and the increasing use of it.

But we should be clear also that, uh, other governments have, in our view, misused urgency. Uh, in our exper We, we experienced this during the Government Blast Government's response to COVID. That was the circumstance in which some use of urgency was clearly understandable, but there were circumstances in which it was used for measures that had major civil liberties implications, such as vaccine passes, where there was no publication of policy papers or a description of how that system would work before parliamentary debate.

And when we made OIA requests, knowing that this was coming up and was going to put through under urgency, government did not respond to provide that information to enable us to analyze the material, let alone inform members of the house on the potential civil liberties implications of, of the system that was being introduced. Um, we also recommend that if a bill is passed under urgency, then standing orders should require the immediate posten enactment scrutiny of that act by a select committee.

And we note that Professor Dean Knight of Victoria, university of Wellington, makes a similar recommendation, uh, in his submission. Um, fundamentally, we think that there's a real problem in New Zealand's political system with select committees being the focus for the public to have their input into policy development laws.

We have a very poor culture of exposure drafts and consultation at earlier stages of, uh, policy and legislative development. And we think that's, that's something, it's obviously outside the remit of the Standing Orders Committee, but where it could do that, where it could make changes to encourage better behavior is to require that when a bill is presented to the house, the government must table a statement which says, what, what, what public consultation has undertaken during development of the policy and the bill specifically if there'd been an exposure

draft of the legislation. Uh, and that there should, should therefore also be a report to the select committee that the bill is referred to, which explains how the government has amended its initial proposals between the exposure draft of the bill and the bill introduced the house. And why, uh, if there's no, if there's been no public consultation on exposure draft the government or the o or the other promoter of Bill, I think it should apply to members Bills too should provide a written statement to the of reasons, to the committee

to explain why no such consultation took place. Be happy to answer the committee's questions. Basically, Andrew, there's a, uh, consistent, uh, consistency of, of opinion in your submission from the ones that we heard last week. And I think, uh, there is, uh, an an awareness that certainly at this table, that, uh, the, uh, separation of powers that should exist, uh, have been come quite blurred, not just in recent times, but over perhaps the last, um, you know, decade or so.

And, uh, given the amount of, uh, submission now coming to select committees, it becomes all the more important that you have a, uh, an independent process of, of the, the executive, uh, for either triaging those, uh, in some particular way, although some sance about the AI sort of model, but, uh, ensuring that there is that, uh, time given to each legislation. Kira McNulty, Thank you. Um, thank you, Mr. Jacobson.

I appreciate your submission and your comments. I agree with your comments around the most recent example, uh, with the Environment Committee and note your suggested amendment to standing orders to reflect that, um, requiring a motion in the house would be one way to address that. At the very least, parliament would get the debate that they would've otherwise got.

Have you got any thoughts on, uh, two possible alternatives? Uh, one being that if a committee resolves your report back early, it needs the full support of the committee, or potentially that it needs the permission of business committee like it does if it was to report back after the date? Um, I think if you, I'm not as okay with how the business committee operates and therefore whether there's government majority control of the business committee, but I think if there's government majority control of the business committee, which wouldn't surprise me, it,

I'm not sure that referral back to the business committee in cases of an early report back, actually, that solves the problem. It just, well, the business part, it passes the responsibility. The Business committee, uh, works by what's called neo unanimity, but it's the current parliament very, very unclear what, uh, neo unanimity is. Right. Okay. I'm not sure referral back to the business committee would solve the problem.

Mm-hmm. Uh, I think, uh, the idea that there should have to be a unanimous vote of the whole select committee for an early report back is one possibility. I can foresee that perhaps government, uh, members might be unhappy with that, and certainly government business managers might be unhappy with that. I, I think it's worthy of consideration, but I'm, I'm actually more inclined to suggest that there needs to be debate on the floor of the house about telman of a select committee, uh, consideration of a bill, rather than it's simply

behind the committee's closed doors. Was there a vote? Did we go this way or that way? I can understand that rationale. Thank you. Uh, one last question, Lawrence. Yeah. Just, uh, supplementary on that, I guess we're looking at two separate things because a report by date might be different from, uh, the the referral date from the house. So what, uh, what happens in terms of the both of the options where, uh, the referral date needs to be something that's debate in the house, but let's say a referral date is six months, at the end,

the select committee chair then still potentially have the mandate to truncate the report back date? Well, Well, that, that's why we said that, um, it shouldn't, the committee should not have the power to shorten it, shorten it substantially, uh, unless there's a debate. Unless there's a debate on how, so before that decision to cur tell the select committee consideration happens and the bill is reported back, yeah.

If the, if the committee chair and perhaps the majority of the committee want to curtail it, then that should be debated in the house before it actually goes through and happens, so that there's at least an opportunity for members to have their say and potentially for a different view to be formed following that debate on potential curtailment. Uh, Mr. Kelson, thank you very much for your submission.
Okay. So it is right on, on the right on point with, uh, another of things. We've already Thank you Very much. Thank you. Thank you. Uh, Franks, Steven Franks and Mark Scan.

This submission is on behalf of the firm and is not on behalf of any client. It reflects the firm's extensive experience dealing with Parliament. I'm a former select committee clerk, former senior advisor to the New Zealand leader of the House, and to a leader of the government, the Senate Australia, and leader of the opposition of the Senate Australia, also published academic articles on perceptions of parliament and on Legisl Legisl speed. Our submission, which I'm sure you've read in detail, has, uh, two main aspects. submissions committees. And Steven will speak to that side. I just wanna start by saying I think there's a risk of being very negative about the New Zealand parliamentary system. And I, I think it's easy to be overly negative. There are great things about the way we legislate New Zealand. We, if the fact that almost every bill goes to select committee is a real standout.

Now in Australia, less than 50% of bills go to select committee. It has to, it requires a positive. No bills are referred by the house representative. Some, some parliaments. They don't send a single bill. Most parliaments is one or two. The Australian Senate sends about half its bills to, um, to a committee. And the average time to pass bills in Australia is 80 days.

So, you know, talk about whether you're going for six months or four months, I mean, start to finish two chambers, 80 days, an increasing number of bills in Australia passed within five days of introduction. That's through both chambers. So, you know, there are things that this committee needs to look at to improve the process in New Zealand, but I'm not part of the view that it's catastrophe.

You know, there are things to improve, but actually we do things pretty well. There is a crunch around time, and it's something that's come through. I've looked at the other submissions and heard people speak. There really is a crunch around time. Part of that is, um, we sit more than Australia about 84 days compared to about 67, and we only pass about half as much legislation a term.

So that's okay. But then when you look at, you know, Canada and the uk, we sit a lot less. Main reason for that, and this is uncomfortable, main reason is everyone else sits on Mondays. There's a reason we don't, New Zealand, we carve it out for cabinet. I mean, that is one of the solutions. Or you could just regulate sitting in the mornings, which was a problem for select committee.

'cause you've got the house competing committee in our submission. I would make another suggestion. It's a suggestion that the committee considered about 20 years ago. Um, it's covered in parliamentary practice in New Zealand as an option. I think you should bring, um, a parallel chamber to do committee of the whole. This works in Canberra with the Federation Chamber, to a lesser extent at Westminster with Westminster Hall, have committee.

The whole debates in the alleged co chamber, whilst the house stages are proceeding in the house, creates about 25% more legislative time without having mps here more weeks of the year, or on Mondays, or sitting every morning. There'll be logistical challenges with it, but, you know, if they can fix it, if they can manage it in Canberra and London, I reckon we could probably manage it here.

So that's my piece. And over to you, Steve. My, um, suggestion, uh, primarily designed to restore what I think is the focus for Facilit committee. It should be the pro procedures should serve the needs of the committee. I think that increasingly they're becoming, um, committees are becoming subject to the needs for political theater.

So that huge numbers of submissions are made as demonstrations of allegiance or fervor, and the role of that process. And the fundamental benefit process is in being warned of unexpected consequences. And I'd say secondary to it, but very similar is for parliament, for the legislature not to be, um, beholden entirely to the executive or to the bureaucracy.

So it's the opportunity to get the outside view, to get the warning, to get the material that will not come, um, through the system or through a Wellington worldview. And that isn't criticizing that, it's just saying it's the way it works. And the risk is that we end up, um, putting in burdens of ritual listening.

Um, people trying to avoid conveying how often they've viewed before the same banal submission or the same expression of view that is unbearable for good people for too long. I think we have, I think the 2002 changes to local government, which obliged, which, which turned it from representative democracy to consultative democracy and forced counselors to spend many, um, days of their lives listening to submissions, which did not add much material, is really partly responsible for the low quality of local government.

And I suggest that Parliament has it in its hands, and committees have it in their hands to state clearly in the standing orders that the primary purpose of hearing submissions is for the committee to learn of things that it otherwise would not learn, and that it will run its procedures to ensure that, that that process does not become subordinated to the others.

I think you should have rules that make mean you do not have to apologize for amalgamating large numbers of similar submissions, or for enabling AI to show you whether there's anything new in any of those. It, it, it needs, um, I won't say it's just re re rhetorical, but the, the standing orders do need to say clearly to people.
Um, don't come here just for political theater.

Just on your, um, written submission in relationship to trialing staff hearing of submissions, um, how would you ensure that staff are able to, um, canvas different political views or, um, angles on relationship to bills? Um, it is a risk, uh, it would be a risk, particularly if staff, um, we're perceived as not as, as a, as not neutral, not apolitical. Um, but you have a situation where it's easy now to recognize a whole lot of people are sort of offered, um, a hearing, but feel they don't get it.

I think the, um, um, biggest value of what you're saying is that, uh, the current system, uh, doesn't produce the best results that you could get. And so we do have to look at, uh, how that would, can I say, uh, marks on, uh, personally interested in that, uh, uh, dual chamber arrangement.

of evidence, at least because I suspect it's just the practice, is the committee has to agree for the submissions to be made available to the public. It's in a worse position. where it's not just a, a simple vote. And, and so my suggestion of separating out urgency motions from the other aspects that currently fall with an urgency. So an urgency motion is set aside the current business of the house, extend the sittings, um, ignore potentially, you know, select committees can't meet and all those things. Uh, trust democracy. Simon Wright, Andrew, is this On call? Yeah. Co koto. Uh, my name's Simon Wright. Um, I'm chair of Tru Trust Democracy. And Andrew, you've already heard from, uh, is also on our committee, so, um, we're gonna do this one together.

Um, but for our oral, we've broken down our recommendations into three themes. Um, so from dependency to independence, uh, from procedural hearings to partic participatory engagement, and another one on how to improve accessibility to information. So the first area concerns major flaws in how democracies operate.

That's, these are flaws that, uh, replicated in democracies around the world, representative democracies, and they're contributing to declining public trust and confidence. So, in essence, many people feel the state doesn't respect them, uh, that their voices aren't heard.

While powerful interests voices are heard, and when people feel that democracy is at risk, um, because success of governments and the public service have been poor at public participation in policy and legislative development, the select committee process has become the main and often the which people be heard.

Um, much of this boils down to the executive's dominant funds. I think the good news is that the passage of the Parliament Bill offers an opportunity to, to address this. So we think the Standing Orders review, uh, should recommend an open public inquiry by a specially constituted select committee to determine the priorities for additional resources. I'm passionate about conservation and the environment, and as a young New Zealander, I have thought deeply about the lineage of many issues which affect my life. Today, I'm particularly interested in legislative scrutiny. The committee has noted a substantial rise in public submissions. I believe it is reasonable that key submissions are selected, and others summarized committees should consider the substantive points raised in submissions, while also taking into account the overall balance of support and operas opposition.

The rise in submissions reflects healthy civic engagement, but it also signals frustration when laws are passed quickly With limited consultation, people look for outlets to express their concerns. This brings me to my key point. I am deeply concerned about the overuse of urgency. This issue isn't new, of course, Jeffrey Palmer warned about it in 1979 that New Zealand had had the fastest law in the West.

The use of urgency rose under the Muldoon governments and has increased again in recent years. Across the past three governments, the share of bills passed under urgency in the first eight months has jumped from 6% to 24% to 36%. The current government has already used urgency without select committees, more than any since at least 1987, excessive urgency, weakened scrutiny, reduces legislative quality and erodes public trust as Forest and Byrd, CEO Nicola Toki put it, it's like drinking coke from a fire hose, just trying

to keep on top of all that reform. Indeed, I felt the same as a first time submitter trying to engage meaningfully with legislation. Moving at such speed. To show the dangers of rushed lawmaking, I want to briefly provide a cautionary tale, which I believe is particularly powerful, and that is the rabbit nuisance act of 1881.

European settlers introduced rabbits to New Zealand in the 1830s, which soon became a major agricultural pest in response to the importation of muids. In this case, stokes weasels and ferrets was widely advocated as a means of control. Despite the warnings of prominent naturalists after initial support and earlier bills seeking to ban the importation of Muids was defeated.

In 1876, after part persistent lo uh, lobbying, the rabbit nuisance bill of 1881 was drafted. This bill proposed protections for enemies of the rabbits keen to clear land for agrarian reform. The executive introduced the bill hot on the heels of a near continuous two weeks, sitting on the heavily contested representation bill fatigued mps provided minimal scrutiny.

The rabbit nuisance bill was introduced on the 6th of September and passed its third reading just three days later. It was approved by the legislative council on the 15th, as predicted, MUIDS wrecked havoc on native wildlife. Today, we call ourselves Kiwis. We adorn our currency with native species. Yet many of these creatures, like Kiwi and kakapo, have been decimated to the point that they have effectively disappeared from our landscapes.

It's clear, it's a clear historical lesson. When lawmaking is too fast, the consequences can last for generations. I believe clear criteria should govern when urgency is justified, reserved only for genuine emergencies, not political convenience. The speaker and consultation with party leaders should determine whether those criteria are met, each time urgency is proposed.

And the longer term, there's room for broader reform to strengthen scrutiny. But the immediate priority must be to tighten the rules around the, the use of urgency. We live in an era where amid profound uncertainty, a misplaced sense of certainty seems to flourish in the spirit of genuine legislative scrutiny. I urge that we preserved within ourselves a measure of doubt, a humility of thought as we look towards the future.

Like many New Zealanders, I care deeply about this place as a young person, I feel it is my duty to do better than previous generations have done for my own.
